key_points: The British introduced new land revenue systems to ensure regular income from agriculture
The Permanent Settlement fixed the land revenue that zamindars had to pay to the British government
Zamindars were made responsible for collecting taxes from peasants, but many lost their lands if they failed to pay revenue
Peasants were often forced to borrow money from moneylenders to pay taxes and farming costs
Indigo was cultivated as a cash crop because it produced blue dye used in European textile industries
European planters forced peasants to grow indigo through advance loans and unfair contracts
Peasants opposed indigo cultivation because it reduced soil fertility and limited food production
examples: Zamindari lands being auctioned when zamindars failed to pay revenue
Peasants borrowing money from moneylenders to pay heavy land taxes
Indigo factories set up by European planters in Bengal
Peasants protesting against indigo cultivation during the Indigo Revolt
formulas: Permanent Settlement → Fixed revenue + Zamindars collect tax from peasants
Indigo cultivation system → Planters give advance → Peasants forced to grow indigo
Peasant hardship → Heavy tax + Debt + Forced crops
exam_tips: Explain how the Permanent Settlement changed rural society
Mention the role of zamindars, peasants and moneylenders
Explain why peasants opposed indigo cultivation
Use examples such as the Indigo Revolt to support answers
